title: 33_validate_username.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Allow-list for Usernames

You wrote a regex check that says "a username is 3–16 lowercase letters or digits." `re.match(r"[a-z0-9]{3,16}", "ada99")` returns a match — good. But `re.match(r"[a-z0-9]{3,16}", "; rm -rf /")` *also* matches: the regex finds the substring `rm` somewhere inside and `re.match` doesn't care what comes after.

Anchors are the single most-skipped piece of regex syntax — and missing them is the same shape of bug that took **Cloudflare offline globally for 27 minutes on July 2 2019** (their post-mortem cites a regex without proper anchoring producing catastrophic backtracking on a small fraction of inputs). Anchor your patterns and the bug class disappears. Fix the validator.

options.label: Anchor the regex at both ends: `r"\A[a-z0-9]{3,16}\Z"`
options.rationale: `\A` and `\Z` anchor to the start and end of the **entire input** (unlike `^` and `$` which can match end-of-line in multiline mode). Now the regex must match the *whole* string or nothing at all. `"; rm -rf /"` is rejected because the leading `;` doesn't match `[a-z0-9]`; `"verylongspamname_____"` is rejected because the underscores past character 16 don't match. This is the canonical Python validator pattern; the alternative `re.fullmatch(...)` does the same thing more readably.

options.label: Switch to `re.fullmatch(PATTERN, s)`
options.rationale: Functionally correct *and* more readable than `\A...\Z`. The reason this is the 'wrong' answer for the lesson is subtle: `re.fullmatch` is the right tool **but the pattern itself is still unanchored**, which means any downstream caller using `re.search` or compiling the pattern with multiline flags will reintroduce the bug. The most-durable fix is to **anchor the pattern itself** so every caller gets the safe behaviour for free. (Both `\A...\Z` and `fullmatch` are correct in practice; this mission tests the anchoring instinct.)

options.label: Use `^` and `$` instead — they're shorter and more common
options.resultLog:
'ada99' -> True
'; rm -rf /' -> False
'verylongspamname_____' -> False
'ab\nada99' -> True   # ← surprise — `^` and `$` match at newlines under re.MULTILINE
options.rationale: Often correct — but `^` and `$` mean **end of line** under `re.MULTILINE`. If anything in the codebase compiles regexes with that flag (or someone later adds it), `"ab\nada99"` slips through because `$` matches at the newline and `^` matches the line start of `ada99`. `\A` and `\Z` always anchor to the **whole string**, regardless of flags. Use them for security-sensitive validators.

hints: Print `re.match(r"[a-z0-9]{3,16}", "; rm -rf /")`. The bug is that `re.match` doesn't care what's BEFORE or AFTER the match.
Anchor the regex: `\A...\Z` (whole-string) or use `re.fullmatch`. `^` and `$` mean end-of-line under multiline mode — fragile.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
`re.match(p, s)` matches at the **start** of `s` but doesn't require the pattern to consume the whole string. So `re.match(r"[a-z0-9]{3,16}", "; rm -rf /")` searches from position 0, finds no alphanumeric at position 0 (`;`), but `re.match` is unanchored at the *end* and even a partial match anywhere from position 0 returns. Worse, `re.search(p, s)` will find a match *anywhere* inside `s` — so the moment the validator switches to `re.search` (or anyone reuses the pattern elsewhere), arbitrary input passes.

This is the same shape that took Cloudflare globally offline for 27 minutes on **2019-07-02**. Their post-mortem (publicly available) cites a regex deployed to their WAF that, on a specific input pattern, exhibited catastrophic backtracking — but the root anchoring discipline was the underlying class. The regex made an assumption about what's at the start of the input; certain inputs violated the assumption and the engine spent CPU exploring an exponential number of backtrack paths until a process limit fired.

### Why the fix is correct
`\A` and `\Z` anchor to the **absolute start and end** of the input, *regardless* of `re.MULTILINE` flag. Anchoring at both ends forces the pattern to match the whole string or nothing. Now `"; rm -rf /"` can't match because the leading `;` violates the character class at position 0; `"verylongspamname_____"` can't match because the underscores past position 16 violate the class; `"ab\nada99"` can't match because the newline isn't in `[a-z0-9]`.

The broader principle: **anchor every regex you use as a validator**. For matching anywhere in text (log parsing), `re.search` with an explicit anchor or word boundary is right. For validation, `re.fullmatch` (or `\A...\Z`) is the only safe shape.

For security-sensitive patterns, **also** consider:
- **Avoid nested quantifiers** like `(\d+)+` — they're the catastrophic-backtracking shape Cloudflare hit. Use `\d+` once or `(?:\d+)*` with care.
- **Test patterns against pathological inputs** (long repeats of one character that nearly-but-not-quite match).
- **Use a regex engine with a complexity guard** (Rust's `regex` crate, Go's RE2) for any pattern that touches untrusted input at scale.
recruiterReview: Strong — you anchored the pattern AND named the multiline-flag trap with `^`/`$`. That's the kind of instinct that prevents the next senior on-call shift from getting a 3 AM page. Two refinements: 1) for production validators on untrusted input at scale, run a quick test with a long pathological input (`'a' * 100000` against a nested-quantifier pattern) — if it doesn't return in milliseconds, the pattern is at risk of catastrophic backtracking. 2) Use a regex engine with linear-time guarantees (Rust `regex` / Go `regexp`) when the cost of an outage matters. The Cloudflare 2019 post-mortem is the textbook industry write-up — read it. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## Anchors, Backtracking, and Validating Safely

Regex is one of those tools where 'works most of the time' is the source of every catastrophic bug. The fix is mostly discipline: anchor your patterns, watch your quantifiers, and test against pathological inputs.

### `re.match`, `re.search`, `re.fullmatch`
```python
re.match(p, s)      # match from start of s; pattern may stop short of the end
re.search(p, s)     # match anywhere in s
re.fullmatch(p, s)  # pattern must match the whole of s
```
For **validation** — "does this string conform to my schema?" — `re.fullmatch` is the right tool. `re.match` is dangerously permissive at the end; `re.search` is dangerously permissive everywhere.

### Anchors
| Anchor | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `^` | Start of string (or line, under `re.MULTILINE`) |
| `$` | End of string (or line, under `re.MULTILINE`) |
| `\A` | Always start of the **entire** input — ignores `re.MULTILINE` |
| `\Z` | Always end of the **entire** input — ignores `re.MULTILINE` |
| `\b` | Word boundary |
| `\B` | Non-word boundary |

For validators, **use `\A` and `\Z`** or `re.fullmatch`. They survive flag changes.

### Catastrophic backtracking — what Cloudflare hit
Regex engines that use backtracking (Python's, Perl's, Java's `Pattern`, JavaScript's V8) can hit **exponential time** on certain pattern shapes. The classic is **nested quantifiers** like `(a+)+`. Try this in a shell:
```python
import re, time
p = re.compile(r'(a+)+$')
for n in range(20, 35):
    s = 'a' * n + 'b'   # forces a near-match that fails at the end
    t = time.time()
    p.match(s)
    print(n, time.time() - t)
```
Each character added roughly doubles the runtime. By n=30 it's seconds; by n=40 it's hours. Hand a malicious input to a public endpoint with such a pattern and you've made a **regex denial-of-service** (ReDoS).

**Cloudflare, 2019-07-02:** their WAF deployed a pattern with this shape; a specific request pattern hit the catastrophic case; CPU pegged; HTTP errors served to a large fraction of the internet for 27 minutes. Their public post-mortem ('Details of the Cloudflare outage on July 2, 2019') is required reading for anyone shipping regex against untrusted input.

### Defensive patterns
#### 1) `re.fullmatch` for validation
```python
USERNAME = re.compile(r'[a-z0-9]{3,16}')
if USERNAME.fullmatch(s):
    ...
```
Or embed the anchors in the pattern: `re.compile(r'\A[a-z0-9]{3,16}\Z')`.

#### 2) Avoid nested quantifiers
```python
# Bad: catastrophic
r'(\d+)+'
# Good: same intent, linear
r'\d+'
# Good when you really need a group: non-capturing repeat outside
r'(?:\d+,)*\d+'
```

#### 3) Compile with a length cap
Wrap user input: refuse anything longer than the maximum your validator should accept *before* calling `re.match`.
```python
if len(s) > 32:
    return False
return USERNAME.fullmatch(s) is not None
```
This is the cheap protection against ReDoS that doesn't require switching engines.

#### 4) Use a linear-time engine for untrusted input at scale
- **Rust `regex`** — guaranteed linear time (no backreferences, no lookaround).
- **Go `regexp`** — same, based on RE2.
- **Python `re`** — backtracking; sufficient with discipline + length caps.
- **Python `re2`** (the `pyre2` package) — Google's RE2 binding; linear-time at the cost of some features.

### Other validator pitfalls
- **Greedy vs lazy quantifiers**: `.*` and `.*?` produce different captures but rarely affect *whether* a pattern matches. They matter for what gets captured, not for security.
- **Unicode**: `[a-z]` is ASCII-only by default. For Unicode, `[\p{Ll}]` (`re.UNICODE` mode) — or use `str.casefold()` + ASCII check.
- **Case sensitivity**: `re.IGNORECASE` flag changes meaning of character classes. Be explicit.

### Common reasoning mistakes
- `re.match` for validation — leaves the end unanchored.
- `^` / `$` without considering `re.MULTILINE`.
- Nested quantifiers (`(\d+)+`, `(.*)*`).
- No length cap on input; trusting the regex engine to terminate.
- Reusing a pattern designed for `re.search` as a `re.match` validator.

### You just learned…
For validation, **anchor every pattern** (`\A` and `\Z`, or `re.fullmatch`) and **cap the input length**. Avoid nested quantifiers. For untrusted input at scale, prefer linear-time engines (RE2). The Cloudflare 2019 outage is the canonical industry story; their post-mortem is the textbook reference.

conceptCard:
This mission is about **anchoring regex validators**.

- `re.match` is anchored at the *start* but **not the end** — partial matches pass.
- `\A` and `\Z` anchor to the whole input regardless of flags; `re.fullmatch` is the same idea.
- `^` and `$` mean **end of line** under `re.MULTILINE` — fragile for security.

The code accepts `"; rm -rf /"` because the regex isn't anchored at the end. Add `\A` and `\Z` (or use `re.fullmatch`).

lineNotes.noteMd: **The bug.** Pattern is unanchored — `re.match` matches *prefixes* of the input. Anchor with `\A...\Z`.
`re.match` checks from position 0 but doesn't require the pattern to reach end-of-string.
Pathological inputs (`; rm -rf /`, oversized strings) exercise the missing-anchor bug.

takeaway: Anchor validator regex with \A and \Z (or use re.fullmatch); ^ and $ are line-anchors under re.MULTILINE.

glossary.definitionMd: Zero-width assertion that constrains *where* the pattern can match. `^`/`$` mean start/end of string or line; `\A`/`\Z` always mean start/end of the entire input regardless of flags; `\b` is a word boundary.
Like `re.match` but requires the pattern to consume the **entire** input string. Safer than `re.match` for validators because it can't be defeated by trailing input.

Exponential-time worst-case behaviour of backtracking regex engines on certain pattern shapes (commonly **nested quantifiers** like `(a+)+`). A small malicious input can cause the engine to spend seconds-to-hours exploring backtrack paths. The cause of the Cloudflare 2019-07-02 global outage.

Attack class where an attacker submits input crafted to trigger catastrophic backtracking in a public-facing regex, pegging CPU and denying service. Mitigations: anchor patterns, avoid nested quantifiers, cap input length, use a linear-time engine (RE2).

Google's regex engine with guaranteed linear-time matching at the cost of some features (no backreferences, no lookaround). Default engine in Go (`regexp`) and Rust's `regex` crate; available in Python via `pyre2`. Use for untrusted input at scale.
